title: Monetary Policy Decision | Speeches | RBA
summary: The RBA left the cash rate unchanged at 3.60% on November 4, 2025, as September quarter trimmed mean inflation rose 1.0% (materially above the 0.6% forecast), driven by both temporary factors (travel costs, council rates, fuel) and persistent components (new dwellings, market services). Governor Michele Bullock noted the Board views the September miss as partly temporary but is closely monitoring whether disinflation continues, with the technical assumption of one more rate cut next year now under greater scrutiny.
key_points: RBA held cash rate at 3.60% (decision was hold only; no rate cut was considered at this meeting)
September quarter trimmed mean inflation rose 1.0% vs 0.6% forecast, driven by new dwellings and market services (persistent components) and temporary factors (travel, council rates, fuel)

Unemployment rate rose to 4.5% monthly, above the RBA's forecast of 4.4% quarter-ended, though forward indicators (vacancies, quits rate) suggest labour market may be more stable than the monthly spike indicates
Board judges there is still a bit of excess demand in the economy and some persistence in inflation
Technical assumption remains one more rate cut in 2026, with inflation expected to return to 2.6% (near 2.5% target) by 2027
Governor noted mixed signals on financial conditions: credit spreads and equity markets suggest easier conditions; mortgage repayments and credit-to-disposable income suggest tighter conditions
why_it_matters: The RBA's hold decision signals caution despite a softening labour market, prioritizing the return of inflation to target over near-term rate cuts. The surprise inflation miss, if sustained, could delay or eliminate the expected rate cut in 2026, affecting Australian dollar direction, bond yields, and mortgage-linked assets. Markets will watch monthly CPI data from December onward for confirmation that disinflation is continuing.
macro_context: Australia's disinflation process appears to have stalled temporarily in Q3 2025. While excess demand is described as modest and labour market tightness is easing, persistent services and dwelling inflation complicate the rate path. The RBA is explicitly watching whether September's miss reflects temporary shocks or a more fundamental re-acceleration in underlying inflation.
notes: Published November 4, 2025, approximately 5 months before today (2026-04-01). Not stale. Source is a primary RBA communication (Governor press conference following Board meeting). Quality is high for macro catalyst analysis; no market structure content present. Q&A section was truncated in extraction but core decision and rationale are fully captured.
